review.notes: Authored as a schema demo; sources not human-verified; reviewer sign-off pending. Restored 2026-07-14 under revised scope: the card teaches the axis, not the phrase. Cue drafted by Claude 2026-07-20 from the entry's meaning and bridge — English situational framing, not a language claim, so it needs editorial taste rather than native review. Override freely.

es.literal: to be on the little bench
es.meaning: To be a substitute; to be benched.
es.example: Se quedó en el banquillo todo el partido.
es.exampleTranslation: He stayed on the bench the whole match.

it.meaning: To be a substitute; to be benched.
it.example: È rimasto in panchina tutta la partita.
it.exampleTranslation: He stayed on the bench the whole match.

bridge.note: Spanish keeps the article — en EL banquillo. Italian drops it — in panchina, bare. This article-dropping after 'in' is systematic in Italian fixed phrases (in centro, in ufficio, in panchina) and it is the single most audible tell of a Spanish speaker.

interference.es_to_it: Spanish speakers say 'nella panchina', importing the article from 'en el banquillo'. The fixed form is 'in panchina', no article.
interference.it_to_es: Italians drop the article and say 'en banquillo'. Spanish requires it: en el banquillo.

cue: How do you say someone is on the bench?
